channel.title: Posts on Adventures in Data Science
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Adventures in Data Science

item.title: The Inevitability of (Missing) Deadlines
item.description: Friday night is date night. I should not promise to show my work when a date with my wife is on the line. While that post is still on the horizon, I also wanted to share more about my ongoing education in R and statistics. Specifically, that I purchased and have begun reading An Introduction to Statistical Learning. I have the e-book version. Like reading Watchmen for the first time, my plan is to read a chapter a night and do those excercises in the day.

item.title: Visualization!
item.description: I’m one of the hosts of All Comics Considered. One of our favorite partner shows is Dorkadia. Given our overlap in staff, topics, and fans, I thought it would be fun to compare our facebook data. So I did. Tomorrow, I’ll share the scripts.

item.title: Marks for Style
item.description: Blogging is old hat to me, but data science is brand new. I’m studying this subject (and a survey of all its componenet parts) like Homer here. This nervous energy is why my first few posts are all about what I do know: Juvenile justice, JEMS, the court, and blogging. Most of these early posts are going to address these topics because it will help establish context. Blogging about what I know does not mean that I’m not honing my R (and github) skill set.

item.title: What We Currently Use
item.description: The best way to move this project forward is to start by explaining the current state of the court’s data and its data analysis. While deep dives into how we document cases and court proceedings are not the most exciting aspect of the project, they are a necessary part of making any lasting change. I’m not sure I’ll be doing my colleagues, and the young people I serve, any favors if this project does not address what does, and does not work, with our current system.

item.title: Setting the Bar for 2018
item.description: This new blog has a single purpose: To document my attempt to deploy a neural network in order to ethically address at least one issue faced by juvenile justice professionals and justice involved youth by December 2018. My name is Marty, and I have been a Cook County Juvenile Probation Officer for over seventeen years 1. Being an officer is not just a job for me. It is a vocation, a calling.
